messages: Hey, I have an interview coming up and I want to prepare.
It's for a mid-level product manager role at a tech company.
Can you put together a single-page prep sheet I can glance at right before the meeting?
I'd like it to list about eight common behavioral questions with short example answers.
Keep each example extremely concise—one or two sentences each—focused on product decisions, stakeholder work, and measuring impact.
Also include three very quick day-of reminders like arrival timing, outfit choice, and a couple of smart questions I could ask.
Plain text is best since I want to paste it into my phone notes without worrying about formatting.
Please keep the entire thing short—around 300 words or fewer so it fits on one screen.
I don't need coding or detailed technical walkthroughs; focus on behavioral examples and product thinking.
That's the core brief — thanks.
Funny thing: last time I overdressed because the interviewer showed up in a hoodie — felt a bit awkward.
I'm debating whether to drive or take the subway; driving means parking stress, subway means relying on schedules.
I have a bad habit of rambling under pressure; someone suggested counting to two before answering to collect myself.
Do people still bring printed resumes? The recruiter already emailed mine, but I don't know if a hard copy looks professional.
At a mock, I froze while trying to sketch metrics for a new feature — spent too long deciding which metric mattered most.
I heard the hiring manager used to work at a competing startup; that makes me want to reference their product history, but I'm not sure that's wise.
Part of me thinks referencing competitors shows preparedness, but another part worries it sounds like homework instead of strategy.
My friends argue about suit colors — someone says navy, someone says charcoal. I just can't decide.
Also wondering if I should bring reference letters; my old manager said they'd write something if needed.
My phone's battery is low and I keep forgetting a charger. Worried I'll need it to navigate to the office.
The recruiter hinted at a short on-site exercise but wouldn't say whether it would be a case or a roleplay.
I tend to over-rehearse answers until they sound scripted. How do people balance practice with sounding natural?
I've listed a few achievements to highlight — one improved retention, another cut feature delivery time — but I'm not sure which will resonate.
I also worry about the room being freezing; maybe I'll wear layers so I can adjust.
My partner keeps telling me to ask about team culture and growth, which seems safe but maybe too generic.
Should salary be left until later? The recruiter hasn't brought it up and I don't want to be premature.
Some blogs suggested sending a handwritten thank-you; seems old-school but could stand out.
It would be nice if there's decent coffee in the office — tiny comforts help when nerves spike.
I should probably update LinkedIn afterward — maybe later tonight I'll add a couple of project bullet points.
Oh, and I might be a bit tired if I don't sleep early the night before; planning to have a calm evening.
Okay, let's wrap this up.
I need something I can open in the lobby and read in about half a minute.
Please keep this non-technical — focus on behavioral and product judgment rather than coding tests or deep technical drills.
Short example replies that show how I'd structure an answer would be ideal, not long anecdotes.
Also toss in a few very quick, practical day-of reminders I can scan in seconds.
Use basic text only—no fancy formatting—so it's easy to paste into my notes without layout issues.
Keep it compact so it fits on one phone screen and doesn't require a lot of scrolling.
Tone it for someone with a couple years of PM experience — not entry-level, not director.
I'll paste whatever you give me into my phone and use it to practice out loud for a few minutes before walking in.
Go ahead and produce that single-sheet prep note now, please.
